nameMeanings.name: De
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Harrell
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: variant of   Harold  .
Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hEarghail ‘descendant of Earghal’, a variant of the personal name Fearghal (see Farrell ).

variants.name: Hairell
meanings: English and Irish: variant of   Harrell  .

variants.name: Arrell
meanings: Irish: from Ó hEarghail, see Harrell .
English: perhaps from an unrecorded Middle English personal name Eryl, a derivative of Old English Eoforwulf.

variants.name: Harrill
meanings: English (Somerset): variant of   Harrell  .

variants.name: Harriel
meanings: Altered form of English   Harrell  .

variants.name: Harold
meanings: English: from the Middle English personal name Harold (Old Norse Haraldr, borrowed from an ancient Germanic form Harjawald). Alternatively, the name may arise from the Middle English personal name Harwold, Harold (Old English Hereweald), particularly in Suffolk, where there was also post-medieval confusion with Hurrell . These all go back to an ancient Germanic personal name composed of the elements heri, hari ‘army’ + wald ‘rule’, which is attested in Europe from an early date; the Roman historian Tacitus records a certain Cariovalda, chief of the ancient Germanic tribe of the Batavi, as early as the 1st century AD See also Herald .
English: habitational name from Harrold (Bedfordshire), from Old English hār ‘gray’ + wald ‘large tract of woodland, high woodland’.

Irish: this name is of direct Norse origin (see 1 above), but is also occasionally a variant of Harrell and Hurrell . Spellings without final d have been adopted as the Anglicized form of the Gaelic Ó hEarghail (see Harrell ).

variants.name: Horrell
meanings: English (Devon):
variant of   Harrell  .

habitational name from Horrel in Werrington (Devon), or perhaps Horrells in Dunchideock (Devon). Horrel in Werrington derives from Old English horh ‘dirt’ + hol ‘hole, hollow’, while Horrells in Dunchideock comes from Old English horh + wella ‘well, spring, stream’.

variants.name: Harrel
meanings: English (London): variant of   Harrell  .

variants.name: Hearrell
meanings: English and Irish: variant of   Harrell  .

variants.name: Hairrell
meanings: English and Irish: variant of   Harrell  .

variants.name: Herrell
meanings: English (Norfolk) and Irish: variant of   Harrell  . This form of the surname is now rare in Britain.

nameMeanings.name: Alice
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Originally a variant of Adelaide , representing an Old French spelling of a reduced form of Germanic Adalheidis. Alice and Adelaide were already regarded as distinct names in English during the medieval period. Alice enjoyed a surge of popularity in the 19th century and periods of favour ever since. It was the name of the central character of Lewis Carroll's Alice's Adventures in Wonderland ( 1865 ) and Through the Looking Glass ( 1872 ), who was based on his child friend Alice Liddell , daughter of the Dean of Christ Church, Oxford.

variants.name: Alys
meanings: Variant spelling of   Alice .

variants.name: Alis
meanings: equivalent of Alice .
